title: Who Is Right About the Impact of a Productivity Boom: Kevin Warsh or the Current FOMC?

Well, investors will also be eagerly
watching the next moves of the Federal
Reserve as the FOMC begins the two-day
April meeting. Let's get into what's
expected from the Fed and the wider
market with Darius Dale. He is 42
Macro's founder and CEO, and also
Christian Hoffman, head of fixed income
at Thornburg Investment Management.
Let's just take a step back because
tomorrow could be Fed Chair Jerome
Powell's last meeting. Christian, I want
to get to you first. What do you make of
that and what do you think is sort of
the moment that everyone will be waiting
for? Will we get sort of any indication
if Fed Chair Jerome Powell will stay on
the Board of Governors?
I don't think we get that on Wednesday,
but I've actually evolved my thought
process on this. I think it I think he
actually sticks around at least till the
end of the year.
It seems like there's a bit of fuzziness
on if the DOJ investigation is getting
it maybe you know, acceptable to some
folks to to confirm Warsh, but I'm not
sure it's going to be acceptable to
Powell.
Um, so I don't think he stays around
forever, but I think the likelihood that
he's around past May 15th is actually
fairly high at this point. Uh, look,
we've gone from really, you know, almost
halfway through the year and and no rate
cuts and that's actually turning out to
be a base case for the rest of the year.
Now, that can change rapidly if the data
changes, but right now the market is
basically pricing in less than a 25%
chance of any cuts for the totality of
2026 and looking at the data today, that
seems right the right call. I think the
most interesting that could come out of
the meeting is really any insights to
any high frequency data on the on the
macro economy,
but broadly I think markets are
preparing for hawkish rhetoric,
which I think is the right call. Now,
the question is how much of that is
being priced in in advance and I think
there actually might be a decent amount,
which is why you're seeing some of the
sell-off today.
Darius, one thing that I do like is that
once we hear from
from the Fed, then we have press
conferences, but it doesn't seem like
that could be the case under Kevin
Warsh. As Christian just had noted, we
sort of hear sort of tea leaves of a
more hawkish tone maybe prior or more
dovish tone.
What would a Fed look like under Kevin
Warsh and just how different would it
would the markets react should there not
be that following press conference?
Oh, thanks for having me, Brooke. So,
the the Fed is going to be very
different under Kevin
Warsh as outlined in his Senate
confirmation testimony. For one, there's
probably going to be a lot less, I
guess, popcorn, if you will, of Fed
speak as it relates to their their their
their outlook for policy and and and and
the economy. So, that's number one, but
to me I think the biggest delta from the
Powell Fed to the Kevin Warsh Fed is a
shift from backward-looking data
dependency CYA monetary policy to
forward-looking data dependency I have a
view and we are going to have a Bayesian
inference process to adjust away from
that view if the data tells us to
monetary policy. Chair Powell was a
fantastic human being uh throughout his
entire time as Fed Chair and and has
guided the country through a lot of, you
know, very difficult times.
However, his track record as Fed Chair
has been been quite poor, if you will.
He's made five major monetary policy
errors since 2018 alone and as a partial
as a function of this backward-looking
data dependency. So, you have to expect
the Kevin Warsh Fed is going to come in,
convince his
colleagues on the FOMC that we are in
fact in a structural uptrend in
productivity, which is something our
research has long argued for and as a
function of that, the Federal Reserve
has a lot more scope on the policy rate
than than than is currently priced in
the markets. Hey Darius, I like that
maybe a reverse-looking versus a
forward-looking Kevin Warsh. Christian,
for you, you are also very focused on
productivity gains, especially when it
comes to AI. How do you think we'll be
viewing this moving forward and maybe
just how different will it be viewed by
the Fed?
I think we're all watching it play out
in real time and I think it's happening,
you know, a lot faster than than people
you know, initially expected.
One of the interesting things is how
it's reverberating through markets in in
a pretty different way through equity
markets and fixed income markets.
Historically, there was this idea that,
you know, fixed income markets react
first, but with AI, which is, you know,
potentially the greatest innovation
we've seen since the internet, you know,
it's really the opposite that, you know,
it has not touched fixed income markets
really until last year
and, you know, continue to more slowly
play out. I think the bigger question
is, you know, how it impacts the the
global macroeconomy
and there are again, I think a strong
case for increased productivity,
which is deflationary, but at the same
time,
you know, that level of investment and
potential GDP growth could push the
neutral rate and real rates to higher
sustainable levels. So, there's some
push-pull there. It's not all good nor
all bad for bonds. That's on the rate
side, you know, on the credit side,
which is equally important, it could be
helpful for the business cycle, but it's
also going to create winners and losers
and we're seeing that increasingly in
equity markets. So, it may make some
businesses more resilient through the
cycle, but it's also going to create
some losers, which should cause pockets
of of credit markets to
to see rising defaults and in private
credit, again, I think it's an easy area
to pick on, but it does seem, you know,
we have credit in the crosshairs of, you
know, some of that innovation and being
on the wrong side of it. All right,
definitely something to look out for.
Something else that caught investors'
minds that we were just chatting a bit
about before, but that report from the
Wall Street Journal that OpenAI did not
meet their internal targets. We're now
seeing Oracle, AMD, CoreWeave fall on
that. Darius, for you, what do you make
of this pullback and is maybe now the
time for investors to get in have they
not been already in on this momentum
that we've seen over the last last
month, really?
Uh, I I would refrain from comment on
very short-term market movements because
I think the goal here is to help
investors retire on time comfortably.
The thing I will say is that if AI is
what we all think it's going to be, then
we're going to see a convergence in the
productivity,
and the valuations between AI exposed,
the AI providers, and the AI
beneficiaries, which is everyone else.
And so, if investors are looking to
allocate fresh capital, they're going to
have to start to look at the other 493
in the S&P, the other companies outside
of our borders, small caps, mid caps,
because essentially what we're doing is
we're transferring technology that has
allowed the AI providers to have
elevated gross margins and elevated free
cash flow growth rates to everyone else
and obviously the technology should have
impacted those business models the same.
But can we go back to this conversation
on productivity before we wrap up
because I think the consensus view as
evidenced by the latest summary of
economic projections is that the
positive productivity shock is going to
lead to a unchanged or moderately
moderately higher neutral rate.
If you think about this from the
perspective of the labor market, in our
opinion, we actually think the neutral
rate is going to drift lower because
it's going to be harder to sustain
employment growth as this technology
diffuses throughout the economy. I'll
leave you with one final stat. If you
break apart the labor market into
different categories and look at true
private services payrolls, which are
payrolls ex-government, ex-healthcare,
ex-social services and education, so
basically everything where the
government is the employer or the
consumer of last resort and just look at
the 52% of the labor market that is true
private services payrolls,
it peaked in March of 2024.
With that being said, Christian, I want
to ask you, we are hearing from Google,
Microsoft, Meta, Amazon, some of these
companies which announced an impact to
their own workforce. They also announced
layoffs. One, what do you hear what are
you waiting to hear from those reports
and two, do you think we'll hear more
about the impact that this does have on
these companies' workforce?
Yeah, I do, but I would I'd be hesitant
to read too much into, you know, some of
this very micro high frequency data.
Again, we're you know, even for the the
major players, again, the OpenAI news
today, I think, you know, caught a lot
of people
you know, by surprise,
but to me it kind of
reminded me of the the mid-90s, right?
When again, it was clear that the
internet was was transformational, but
the there was a lot of the investment
that turned out to be to be
malinvestment, particularly on the
infrastructure side and then the
ultimate winners, you know, weren't
decided for for a number of years,
right? For for a while it was Yahoo, you
know, AltaVista was a name that, you
know, was terribly exciting for folks,
you know, for a long time, but you know,
Google became the ultimate winner, you
know, at the end of the day and
look, you know, six months ago, I think,
you know, OpenAI was was the industry
darling and, you know, right now it
seems like there's a you know, strong
preference for for Anthropic and Claude
and again, this is playing out very
quickly and and in real time
and I'd be hesitant to extrapolate, you
know, too much from from any one day,
but I think history can teach a lot of
lessons on this. Christian, are you
saying that we're already seeing the
winners and the losers here already?
I'm just saying it's too early to
declare winners and losers

>> and you know, what the whole competitive
landscape looks like two years from now
might be unrecognizable from from what
we're looking at today. All right, we'll
